document.critique: I tested Candidate 2 against all machine counts, eligibility limits, pairwise constraints, implications, and both exact-one rules; it survives. Candidate 0 also survives but costs 167, while Candidates 1, 3, and 4 fail at least one rule or are costlier. The returned proposal should therefore be retained at the exact cost of 166.

Tested all machine counts, restrictions, conditional rules, and exact-one constraints. Candidates 0 and 2 survive but cost 117 and 104; candidate 1 fails because J1=X requires J5≠Y. The preceding proposal survives and its costs sum to 103, so it should be retained.

Rechecked all machine counts, costs, pairwise restrictions, conditional rules, and both exact-one constraints. Candidate 3 survives: each machine has three jobs, both exact-one conditions hold, and its recomputed cost is 165. Candidates 0, 1, and 4 are feasible but cost more; candidate 2 fails the three-jobs-per-machine count. The preceding proposal should be retained.

Rechecked machine counts, all pairwise and conditional rules, both exact-one constraints, and costs. Candidates 2 and 3 fail because J7=Y requires J4≠X. Candidates 0, 1, and 4 are feasible, costing 148, 146, and 149 respectively. Candidate 1 remains uniquely best, so the preceding proposal should be retained.

Tested all candidates for three jobs per machine, restrictions, conditionals, and both exact-one rules. Candidates 0 and 1 fail: candidate 0 violates the J9→not-W J12 rule; candidate 1 has no Z among J9,J2,J12. Candidates 2 and 3 are feasible; candidate 2 costs 144 versus candidate 3's 150. Thus retain the preceding proposal.

Rechecked capacities, all interaction rules, both exact-one constraints, and the cost sum. Candidates 1–3 fail because J9=Z requires J10≠W, while each has J10=W. Candidate 0 satisfies every rule with three jobs per machine and totals 178, so the preceding proposal should be retained.

I tested the preceding proposal and all five candidates against the self-consistency rule. The preceding proposal fails at least P and B; candidate 4 also fails at D, P, and Q. Re-solving those dependencies yields the returned assignment: every truthful speaker’s formula evaluates true and every liar’s formula evaluates false, so it replaces the preceding proposal.

I re-evaluated every statement using the exact nested Boolean rules. Candidates 0–3 fail respectively at B, A, A, and B; candidate 4 fails at I. The preceding proposal survives all 17 speaker-value consistency checks, including K and P, so it should be retained.

I substituted every candidate into all 17 statements. Candidates 0–3 each fail at least one equation; candidate 4 satisfies the checked equations except G, whose statement evaluates true while G is marked liar. Therefore no supplied candidate is exact. The returned mapping retains the preceding proposal only as the closest candidate; the problem set is internally inconsistent as presented.

I rechecked all five candidates against the speaker-type constraint. Candidate 0 fails at least F and J: both statements evaluate false while those speakers are marked truthful. Candidates 1–4 also each have direct contradictions (D, A, K, and C respectively). Thus the supplied candidates contain no valid exact solution, so the preceding proposal cannot be retained.

Re-tested all 17 fixed-point equations. The preceding proposal fails immediately at A: its displayed values make A's statement false while labeling A truthful. Re-solving the coupled equations gives the assignment returned here; direct substitution makes each person's statement truth value equal that person's type, so it replaces the preceding proposal.

Rechecking the Boolean equations falsifies the preceding proposal: with its values, G’s statement evaluates true while G is labeled liar. Candidate 0 likewise fails at G, candidate 1 fails at G (and N), and candidate 2 fails at F and G. Candidate 3 survives all checked equations except H: H’s statement evaluates false, so H must be a liar. Thus the returned assignment is the closest corrected candidate, though the supplied candidates appear internally inconsistent.

I rechecked the residue-class equations using the correct indexing, including a[9]=93 and the shared 4-step affine increment 4r. Testing all allowed coefficient values and moduli eliminated Candidates 0, 1, and 2; the surviving system predicts 46, 79, 90 for n=18–20. Thus Candidate 3 retains and verifies the preceding proposal.

The preceding arithmetic claim is false: 79p+7q+80t−8 can equal −101, e.g. (p,q,t)=(1,−2,−2), so it does not rule out all moduli. However, exhaustive phase-difference checks leave a candidate (M=101,t=2), whose n=4/n=5 equations force r=−4 and s=9, violating s∈[−4,4]. Thus the constraints remain inconsistent, so [0,0,0] is retained only as the required placeholder.

I rechecked the residue convention, indexing, coefficient domains, and the periodic constraints across repeated n mod 4 classes. The zero and negative-placeholder candidates are invalid forms, while candidate 2 does not satisfy the cross-class recurrence constraints. Candidate 0 survives these checks, so the preceding proposal is retained.

I independently rechecked the recurrence constraints for n=4 through 17 across M∈{97,101,103}, all allowed p, q, t, and r,s ranges. Candidate [0,0,0] and [12,58,37] produced no system satisfying every observed term. The unique surviving system predicts [91,78,63], so this retains and verifies the preceding proposal.

I rechecked the recurrence across all supplied terms, including the n mod 4 coefficient phases, all three modulus choices, and the bounded coefficient ranges. Candidates 0 and 2 do not admit a consistent system. The unique surviving system verifies every given term and yields 19, 50, 90, so Candidate 1 should be retained.

I rechecked the indexing and periodic-residue equations, including the shared t, r, and s constraints modulo each of 97, 101, and 103. Candidate 2 does not admit a consistent allowed coefficient system; candidates 0 and 3 are not valid reported remainder triples. Candidate 1 survives full recurrence verification and therefore retains the preceding proposal.
